followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: Added two new technical objections from codex's review: (1) the Coxeter eigenvalue characterization via 'totatives of h' is incorrect (should use Coxeter exponents; confirmed for A_3); (2) the even/Type II lattice definition is misstated (self-norms even, not all pairwise products even). Added the scale-factor/units gap as a moderate objection. Confirmed my dimensional inconsistency objection in Sec. V.1.3 is real and codex/gemini both note it (codex labels it major, gemini minor—I maintain major). Overall score and verdict unchanged at 6.0 / promising.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: Codex's claim that 'total irrationality does not follow from non-crystallographic symmetry' is overstated. The Salem-number structure of the Coxeter element's eigenvalues (λ > 1 real, others on the unit circle with irrational angles) makes total irrationality straightforward: the discrete orbit of any putative lattice vector in E_ph under the Coxeter element would be infinite and unbounded (for the expanding eigenspace) or an infinite irrational orbit of fixed length (for the rotating eigenspaces), both contradicting lattice discreteness. The gap is presentational, not mathematical.

Codex's verdict 'contested' appears too strong. The core mathematical construction is logically coherent; the gaps identified are in exposition (missing hypotheses) and the speculative Section V, not in the fundamental sC&P framework.

Gemini's technical soundness score of 8 is too generous. The Coxeter-exponent error, the even-lattice definition error, the scale-factor/units gap, and the dimensional inconsistency in Sec. V.1.3 collectively justify a lower soundness score.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: Codex: The Coxeter eigenvalue statement in Sec. II.4 ('primitive h-th roots where m_j runs over totatives of h') is incorrect; the correct statement uses Coxeter exponents, which need not all be coprime to h (e.g., A_3 has exponent m=2 with gcd(2,4)=2, yet e^{2pi i 2/4}=-1 is a genuine eigenvalue).

Codex: The definition of an even (Type II) lattice is misstated. The paper requires all pairwise inner products to be even, but the standard definition requires only self-inner-products (norms) to be even. E_8 roots have pairwise inner product -1 between adjacent roots yet E_8 is even.

Codex: The identification of scale factors with the full unit group of the maximal real subfield K_R is not proved; not every unit need be realized as an automorphism of the chosen lattice preserving the splitting.
Codex: The no-tiling argument (Sec. IV.3.1) implicitly assumes finite-local-complexity / finite-prototile tilings, a hypothesis that should be stated explicitly.
Codex: The window radial-symmetry conclusion (Sec. IV.2.3) requires regularity assumptions on the window class (closed, measurable-regular) that are not stated.
Gemini: High novelty and clarity of the mathematical sections (I-IV) confirmed by an independent reviewer.

main_claims.claim: Quasicrystals with self-similarity can be systematically generalized from Euclidean to Lorentzian spacetime via a symmetric cut-and-project scheme based on hyperbolic Coxeter theory.
main_claims.notes: The three-step construction (Lorentzian lattice; decomposition invariant under Coxeter-element subgroup; rotationally-symmetric window or weighting function) is clearly laid out and logically consistent with the Euclidean framework in Section III.

main_claims.claim: The non-crystallographic orientational symmetry group of a spacetime quasicrystal is infinite, because hyperbolic Coxeter elements have infinite order.
main_claims.notes: Correct: the Coxeter number is finite for finite/spherical groups but infinite for hyperbolic groups. The group generated by the Coxeter element therefore has infinite order in the Lorentzian case. This is a genuine and important distinction.

main_claims.claim: The largest eigenvalue of a bicolorable hyperbolic Coxeter element is a Salem number.
main_claims.notes: Attributed to A'Campo (1976) [Ref. 1]. The claim is standard in the Salem-number/hyperbolic-Coxeter literature. The spectral description in Sec. IV.1.3 (reciprocal pair with λ > 1, remaining eigenvalues on the unit circle) is consistent with this.

main_claims.claim: Spacetime quasicrystals cannot have local scale invariance, cannot be promoted to finite-prototile tilings, and have no preferred window size.
main_claims.notes: No tiling (IV.3.1): the geometric argument implicitly assumes finite-local-complexity/finite-prototile tilings; this hypothesis is not stated. No local scale invariance (IV.3.3): uses the LIDS criterion from [7,5] but does not prove the ball-window impossibility rigorously. No preferred window size (IV.3.2): asserted, not proved.

main_claims.claim: Spacetime quasicrystals can be self-dual or globally scale-invariant via the weighting scheme.
main_claims.notes: Follows naturally from the Euclidean framework in Sections III.1.3 and III.3.6 applied to the Lorentzian case. The preference for the weighting over the window scheme is well-motivated by the absence of a preferred window size.

main_claims.claim: 4D Minkowski spacetime can be embedded as an irrationally-sloped dense, non-self-intersecting slice of the 10D Lorentzian torus T^{10} = R^{9,1}/II_{9,1}.
main_claims.notes: The mathematical claim that the irrational 4D subspace does not intersect the lattice (except at the origin) follows from non-crystallographicity. The claim that observers would experience 'no closed timelike curves' is asserted without proof; the causal structure of the restricted metric requires separate analysis absent from the paper.

main_claims.claim: A 'volume seesaw' with n=6 extra dimensions reproduces the observed hierarchy M_{EW}^2 ~ M_{Pl} * M_Lambda.
main_claims.notes: Eq. (59) equates [L]^{3+n} to [L]^n, which is only dimensionally consistent if [L]^3 = 1 (i.e., implicitly in Planck units, but this is not explicitly adopted and not justified by the text). The authors do not show the algebraic steps from (62)+(64) to (65); an independent combination does not cleanly reproduce the stated seesaw for n=6.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Uniform filling (ergodic equidistribution) of the extra-dimensional torus by the thickened 3-brane, needed to justify the volume seesaw V_{3D} * V_perp = V_T.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The equation implicitly assumes the thickened 3-brane tiles the torus without overlap. Equidistribution follows from irrationality asymptotically, but the rate determines whether the finite-thickness, finite-volume version of the seesaw is a good approximation or an exact identity.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The higher-dimensional Planck mass M_D equals the electroweak scale M_{EW} (no new hierarchy introduced at the fundamental level).
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: This assumption restates the goal (explaining the Planck/EW hierarchy) rather than deriving it. If M_D != M_{EW}, the seesaw argument fails. The paper imports this from ADD [Ref. 4] without noting it is circular.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The de Sitter horizon volume H^{-3} equals the finite 3-volume of the 3-brane.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The de Sitter horizon is an observer-dependent concept in GR; the 3-brane volume is a geometric property of the embedding. Equating them requires a physical mechanism that is absent from the paper.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Standard Model fields are exactly confined to the irrationally-sloped 4D subspace while gravity propagates in the full 10D bulk.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: No confinement mechanism is derived from the quasicrystal structure. Without one, the ADD-style dimensional reduction (Eq. 63) cannot be applied, and the physical picture is incomplete.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The Coxeter element chosen as the generator of the non-crystallographic subgroup is the most natural or unique choice; other choices might yield different scale factors and hierarchy predictions.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The paper notes that classifying all non-crystallographic subgroups of Aut(Λ) is left for future work. Physical conclusions in Section V depend on the specific Coxeter-element choice and the specific II_{9,1} eigenspace splitting. Alternative choices could give different physical outputs.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does the dimensional analysis in Section V.1.3 (Eq. 57-65) produce the seesaw M_{EW}^2 ~ M_{Pl} * M_Lambda for n=6 when worked through consistently in Planck units? What implicit unit convention makes Eq. (59) dimensionally consistent?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The paper omits intermediate algebraic steps from (62)+(64) to (65). An ADD phenomenologist should verify whether a self-consistent reading of the equations reproduces the stated seesaw, and if so, what unit conventions are being used.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: For the bicolorable hyperbolic Coxeter groups associated with I_{3,1} and II_{9,1}, does A'Campo's theorem (Ref. [1]) directly imply the leading Coxeter-element eigenvalue is a Salem number, and are all stated eigenvalue numerics correct?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: A'Campo (1976) works in a specific algebraic setting. Verifying the hypotheses for these specific groups, and independently checking the numerical eigenvalues given for the 3x3 and 10x10 Coxeter matrices, requires symbolic computation or access to the original references.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Are the Coxeter-Dynkin diagram corrections to Vinberg's original papers (claimed in Appendix A for p=17-19) correct, and do the fundamental root tables reproduce the diagrams accurately?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The original papers are in Russian/German and some are difficult to obtain. Independent recomputation of Vinberg's algorithm for these cases is needed to confirm the claimed corrections.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the 'first examples' claim for Lorentzian spacetime quasicrystals accurate against the full model-set and mathematical quasicrystal literature, including unpublished or conference proceedings work?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: A comprehensive literature search across model-set theory, Lorentzian lattice theory, and mathematical physics is required beyond what can be assessed from the supplied references.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is there a physically consistent mechanism, derivable from or consistent with the spacetime quasicrystal framework, that confines Standard Model matter to the irrationally-sloped 4D subspace?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This is a model-building question in quantum field theory and string theory. The paper provides no confinement mechanism; evaluating whether one can be constructed requires expertise in extra-dimensional field theory.

novelty_assessment: The mathematical core of the paper is genuinely novel. While Lorentzian/hyperbolic Coxeter theory and self-dual Lorentzian lattices are well-established, their systematic application to construct quasicrystal analogues in Minkowski spacetime—with careful treatment of the cut-and-project scheme, self-duality, global scale invariance, and Salem numbers—appears to be new. The paper correctly distinguishes its construction from prior works on 'time quasicrystals' (Flicker 2018, Vacaru 2018, etc.). The identification of the relevant non-crystallographic group as the infinite group generated by a hyperbolic Coxeter element, and the resulting novel features (infinite orientational symmetry, no tiling, no local scale invariance, only global scale invariance), constitute a coherent new mathematical framework. The notions of self-dual and globally-scale-invariant quasicrystals via the weighting scheme also appear to be new for the Euclidean case (Sec. III). The physical speculations in Section V reuse the framework of large extra dimensions and toroidal compactifications (Moore 1993, ADD 1998), with the quasicrystal structure providing a new geometric motivation, but without deriving quantitative predictions beyond plausibility arguments. Both other reviewers agree on high novelty; the 'first examples' claim should be verified against the full model-set literature.
score_rationale: The mathematical sections (II-IV) are the paper's strength: the Lorentzian sC&P construction is logically coherent, the first examples are explicitly computed, and the novel features (infinite symmetry group, no tiling, no LIDS, global scale invariance) are plausibly argued. Novelty is real and confirmed by all reviewers. Technical soundness is nudged down from my cycle-1 score of 6 to 5.5, reflecting the additional errors surfaced in this cycle: the Coxeter-exponent/totatives mismatch in Sec. II.4 (a genuine but secondary error), the even-lattice definition error in Sec. II.1, and the unproved scale-factor/units identification in Appendix B. These join my cycle-1 objections about the dimensional inconsistency in the volume seesaw (Sec. V.1.3) and the absence of CTC analysis (Sec. V.1.1). The seesaw derivation remains the paper's weakest point as a concrete physical claim; the dimensional issue is real and the n=6 conclusion is not transparently derived. Clarity is 7 rather than higher because the mathematical sections are well-written but the errors in Sec. II.1 and II.4 indicate that background definitions are not all carefully checked. The paper remains 'promising' rather than 'strong': the mathematical framework is a genuine contribution, the errors are correctable, and the speculative physics is appropriately flagged, but it is not field-shaping until the mathematical gaps (scale-factor/units, LIDS impossibility proof) are closed and the seesaw argument is either fixed or retracted.
summary: Boyle and Mygdalas extend self-similar quasicrystal theory from Euclidean space to Minkowski spacetime via a Lorentzian symmetric cut-and-project scheme built on hyperbolic Coxeter theory, Lorentzian self-dual lattices, and the spectral theory of hyperbolic Coxeter elements. The paper is mathematically novel and coherent at its core: the key features of Lorentzian quasicrystals (infinite orientational symmetry, no tiling, no local scale invariance, global scale invariance) are plausibly argued and represent genuine new mathematical territory. Section V is speculative throughout and the hierarchy seesaw argument contains a dimensional inconsistency not resolved by the authors. After auditing the other reviewers, I identify several additional presentational errors (Coxeter eigenvalue characterization via totatives, even-lattice definition) and one substantive gap (scale-factor/unit identification), but maintain that the overall mathematical construction is sound and the verdict should be "promising."

technical_objections.evidence: Eq. (57): V_perp = l_Pl^n (units [L]^n). Eq. (58): V_T = R^n (units [L]^n). Eq. (59): V_{3D} * V_perp = V_T implies [L^3][L^n] = [L^n], requiring [L^3] = 1. No unit system is specified that would make this consistent non-trivially.
technical_objections.objection: The volume seesaw equation (59), V_{3D} * V_perp = V_T, is dimensionally inconsistent as written. V_{3D} ~ H^{-3} has units [length]^3 and V_perp = l_Pl^n has units [length]^n, so their product has units [length]^{3+n}, while V_T = R^n has units [length]^n. The equation as written requires [length]^3 = 1.

technical_objections.evidence: Eq. (62) gives (from volume seesaw + Friedmann): H^{-3} l_Pl^n = R^n. Eq. (64, ADD): M_{Pl}^2 = M_D^{n+2} R^n = M_{EW}^{n+2} R^n. Combining to eliminate R^n and setting n=6 does not manifestly reproduce M_{EW}^2 = M_{Pl} * M_Lambda without further assumptions not stated in the text.
technical_objections.objection: The claim that n=6 extra dimensions reproduces the seesaw M_{EW}^2 ~ M_{Pl} * M_Lambda is not verified by explicit algebra. The paper does not show the steps from Eqs. (62) and (64) to the stated result (65); an independent combination yields a different scaling.

technical_objections.evidence: Standard result (Humphreys, Reflection Groups and Coxeter Groups): eigenvalues of the Coxeter element are e^{2pi i m_j / h} for Coxeter exponents m_j. For A_3: exponents {1,2,3}, totatives of 4 are {1,3}. The exponent m=2 is excluded by the totatives condition but corresponds to eigenvalue -1 which is correct.
technical_objections.objection: Section II.4 incorrectly states that the eigenvalues of a finite Coxeter element are the 'primitive h-th roots of unity where m_j runs over the totatives of h.' The correct statement uses Coxeter exponents m_j, which need not all be coprime to h. For example, A_3 has h=4 and Coxeter exponents {1,2,3}; m=2 is not a totative of 4 but e^{2pi·i·2/4} = -1 is a genuine eigenvalue.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section II.4, paragraph beginning 'For finite W, the eigenvalues of c...'

technical_objections.evidence: In E_8, the roots satisfy r_i · r_i = 2 (even) but adjacent roots in the Dynkin diagram satisfy r_i · r_j = -1 (odd). E_8 is universally acknowledged as an even lattice, but the paper's definition would exclude it.
technical_objections.objection: Section II.1 misstates the definition of an even (Type II) lattice. The paper says 'the inner product of any two points in Λ is an even integer.' The correct definition is that every self-inner-product v·v is an even integer. Pairwise inner products between distinct lattice vectors need not be even (e.g., in E_8, adjacent roots have inner product -1).
technical_objections.paper_location: Section II.1, paragraph defining Type II lattices

technical_objections.evidence: The paper defines inflation factors as units of K_R and states this without proving that each such unit corresponds to a lattice automorphism preserving the decomposition. The lattice automorphism group may be a proper subgroup of the unit group action.
technical_objections.objection: The identification of scale factors with the full unit group of the maximal real subfield K_R of the Coxeter splitting field is asserted but not proved. Not every unit of K_R need be realized as an automorphism of the chosen lattice that preserves the sC&P splitting.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section III.3.4 and Appendix B, paragraph defining K and K_R

technical_objections.evidence: The argument shows that a tile T would require infinitely many orientations (since G is infinite), which prevents a finite-prototile symmetric tiling. But a tiling with infinitely many prototile types is not ruled out without additional hypotheses explicitly stated.
technical_objections.objection: The no-tiling argument in IV.3.1 implicitly requires finite-local-complexity or finite-prototile hypotheses that are not stated. Infinite-prototile tilings with infinite orientational symmetry are not excluded by the argument as written.

technical_objections.evidence: Invariance under a single irrational rotation r (generating a dense subset of SO(2) in the limit) forces radial symmetry only for closed or measurable-regular sets. The paper does not specify the function class for weighting functions or the topology for windows.
technical_objections.objection: The claim that an invariant window must be a radially-symmetric disk in each internal Coxeter plane (Choice 3, Sec. IV.2.3) requires regularity assumptions on the window that are not stated. Under an irrational rotation, arbitrary measurable invariant sets can be non-radial unions of orbits.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper states observers 'would regard themselves as living in infinite (non-compact) 4D Minkowski spacetime, with no closed timelike curves' but provides no argument about the induced causal structure on the 4D slice.
technical_objections.objection: The absence of closed timelike curves for observers confined to the 4D irrationally-sloped slice of the 10D torus is asserted but not analysed. The 10D torus with Lorentzian metric globally contains CTCs; whether any geodesic of the restricted 4D metric closes up requires separate causal analysis.
